# Fundamental Corpus for Mia — 1:1 Certification Version

## Q4. RQTH Accommodations
Julien is recognized as RQTH. He needs a healthy, calm, stable environment, with consideration for fatigue. For more details, he can be contacted via the contact form on his website.

## Q7. Management
Julien does not wish to move into management. He prefers to focus on the mission, solving complex technical problems, and technical expertise.

## Q9. Exact Definition of SVDD, SPH SVDD, and Deep SPH SVDD
The Support Vector Data Description (SVDD) method aims to find the center and radius (c, ρ) parameters of the smallest enclosing hypersphere for a set of points. Applied to anomaly detection, the goal is to keep normal points inside the sphere and anomalies outside. There are two machine learning approaches: Deep soft-boundary SVDD and One Class Deep SVDD, both based on this principle.
In Julien's thesis, two methods are proposed: SPH SVDD and Deep SPH SVDD. The idea of SPH SVDD is to reformulate the SVDD problem using a hypersphere in conformal geometric space, i.e., to use a hyperspherical layer to solve the optimization problem with conformal geometric algebra. The network then contains a hyperspherical layer with a single neuron (one sphere) and the inputs x. The Deep SPH SVDD method is an extension to the case of multiple spheres. The main idea is to efficiently enclose specific groups of normal points in feature space with a group of spheres. This aims to improve detection accuracy and model interpretability, especially for multimodal data.
Keywords: SVDD, hypersphere, center, radius, anomaly, inside, outside, soft-boundary, one class, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, conformal geometric algebra, multi-spheres, groups of points, features, interpretability, multimodal data.
chunks.header: Q9. Exact Definition of SVDD, SPH SVDD, and Deep SPH SVDD
chunks.keywords: SVDD, hypersphere, center, radius, anomaly, inside, outside, soft-boundary, one class, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, conformal geometric algebra, multi-spheres, groups of points, features, interpretability, multimodal data.

chunks.text:
## Q16. Academic Background
Julien studied mathematics from high school, then at the University of La Rochelle (Bachelor's, Master's, CAPES). He taught mathematics at Saint-Exupéry High School in La Rochelle. He completed laboratory internships (automatic tide gauge at LIENSs, trajectory analysis at XLIM/MIA, orbit interpolation at SYRTE). He defended a PhD in applied computer science for artificial intelligence, where he published and presented his work at various conferences.

## Q19. English
Julien speaks English at the minimum level required to work and communicate at international conferences. His writing is proficient.

## Initialization Method
A good initialization method for hyperspherical layers is essential to ensure convergence and learning stability. The method proposed in the thesis allows chaining several hyperspherical layers, which is not possible with standard initialization (like Glorot). It relies on asymptotic properties of generalized Gamma distributions and covariance calculations adapted to the geometric structure of spheres.

## Datasets Used
The main datasets used to validate the Deep M-SPH SVDD method are:
- Synthetic point sets (to illustrate the behavior of hyperspherical layers)
- MNIST
- CIFAR-10

## Exploratory/Theoretical Component of the Thesis
The thesis includes a significant theoretical component, notably:
- An approximation theorem for hyperspherical layer networks
- Mathematical study of convergence and stability properties
- Analysis of the collapse phenomenon in Deep SVDD models

## Reference Persons
- Prof. Catherine Choquet (MIA lab director)
- Christophe Saint-Jean (thesis advisor)

## RQTH Needs
Julien is recognized as RQTH. He needs a healthy, calm, stable environment, with consideration for fatigue. For more details, he can be contacted via the contact form on his website.

## Technical Preference / Management
Julien does not wish to move into management. He prefers to focus on the mission, solving complex technical problems, and technical expertise.

## [Passion] Astronomy and astrophotography
Astronomy is a central passion of Julien. He practices astrophotography (photographing the sky, celestial bodies and events) and built his Orbitalis project (orbitalis.fr) around this passion — an astronomy journal listing observable events. His master's in Astronomy & Physics at the Paris-Meudon Observatory and his internship at SYRTE (satellite orbits) reflect a long-standing interest in the sky. ## [Personality] Values and way of working
Julien places scientific ethics, rigor and honesty at the heart of his commitments. Passionate about technology and AI, he is tenacious: he never gives up and sees his projects through. His main point of vigilance, which he owns with perspective, is his high standards: a strong drive to do things well and understand every detail. He has turned this into an asset by learning to calibrate his level of detail to what is at stake, to prioritize by impact and to meet deadlines — which makes him both rigorous and efficient, especially in a team.

chunks.text:
## [Project] Agentic AI platform for HR — Unapei 17 (current role)
Since April 2026, Julien has been AI Project Lead at Unapei 17 (La Rochelle, France). He leads and builds an agentic-AI platform serving HR and accounting data. Core of the project: an autonomous AI agent that answers HR teams' questions in natural language by querying the databases itself (text-to-SQL / NL2DB), with no technical skills required. It is a true agent that reasons and decides (Reason-Act-Observe loop, sub-goal decomposition, schema linking), not a scripted interface. He also designed and developed a multi-database semantic-matching algorithm that links heterogeneous databases (HR, accounting, activity) into a unified, auditable, explainable foundation (code computes the facts, the LLM reasons over them: no hallucination, reproducible results, traceable reasoning). The whole system is fully local (Ollama, SQLite, LangGraph, MCP): no data leaves the organization, with GDPR being fundamental since Unapei handles sensitive data.
Keywords: Unapei 17, AI project lead, current role, agentic AI, autonomous agent, NL2DB, text-to-SQL, semantic matching, GDPR, HR, accounting, LangGraph, MCP, Ollama, local, company, concrete, LLM.
chunks.header: [Project] Agentic AI platform for HR — Unapei 17 (current role)
chunks.keywords: Unapei 17, AI project lead, current role, agentic AI, autonomous agent, NL2DB, text-to-SQL, semantic matching, GDPR, HR, accounting, LangGraph, MCP, Ollama, local, company, concrete, LLM.

MIA is the conversational AI assistant designed and built by Julien (she is the one answering here). She presents his background and research to recruiters, researchers and the curious. Technically, she combines a large language model (LLM) with RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) technology to draw on a documentary corpus (profile, thesis, publications, projects) and provide relevant, personalized answers. The project shows Julien's ability to design a complete AI solution, from architecture (Node.js backend, RAG, security) to production.

## [Project] Orbitalis — dynamic astronomy journal (orbitalis.fr)
Orbitalis (orbitalis.fr) is a personal project of Julien: a dynamic astronomy journal that automatically extracts, from sources and media, information about observable astronomical events (eclipses, conjunctions, meteor showers, transits…) for amateur astronomers. It combines his passions — astronomy and astrophotography — with his expertise in information processing and AI. It is also the platform hosting his other web projects (orbitalis.fr subdomains).

## [Project] Singapore 360° — immersive gallery (singapore360.orbitalis.fr)
Singapore 360° (singapore360.orbitalis.fr) is a personal site by Julien that relives a trip to Singapore in full immersion: more than 90 interactive 360° panoramas across 21 locations, a cinematic 360° video of about 5 minutes covering the must-see spots (temples, Sentosa beaches, nature reserves), and a gallery of more than 260 photographs sorted by theme (aviation, wildlife, temples, urban scenes). All photos are Julien's own. Interface in French, English and Chinese; no tracking cookies, no personal data collected. The project demonstrates his skills in web development, photography and immersive experience.
Keywords: Singapore360, Singapore, 360, panorama, VR, immersive, travel, photography, gallery, 360 video, web development, personal project, orbitalis.

chunks.text:
## [Project] JobScope / Jobora — AI-powered job search (jobscope.orbitalis.fr)
JobScope (also called Jobora, jobscope.orbitalis.fr) is an AI-assisted job-search platform built by Julien. It helps candidates optimize their applications: ATS analysis with a compatibility score (0-100%) between a CV and a job offer, detailed report, AI-generated cover letters, real-time analysis and PDF export. It also offers a "swipe" job-discovery mode (like a dating app), a centralized dashboard, automatic job scraping (France Travail) and AI keyword extraction from the CV. Payments via Stripe. The project shows Julien's ability to build a complete AI product, from user need to monetization.
Keywords: JobScope, Jobora, job, job search, ATS, compatibility score, cover letter, CV, swipe, France Travail, scraping, Stripe, product, SaaS, AI, project.

chunks.text:
## [Project] Research contributions: Deep M-SPH SVDD and initialization method
Beyond the thesis, Julien's two flagship research contributions are: (1) the Deep M-SPH SVDD model, a multi-sphere hyperspherical approach to anomaly detection based on conformal geometric algebra; (2) a dedicated initialization method for hyperspherical-layer networks, improving convergence and stability and enabling layer stacking. See the thesis knowledge base for details.
Keywords: Deep M-SPH SVDD, multi-sphere, initialization, anomaly detection, contribution, research, hyperspherical, convergence, stability.

## [Project] Concrete applications developed
Julien developed several concrete computer-vision and signal-processing applications: an automated visual tide gauge (automatic reading of a tide-scale image, LIENSs laboratory), the characterization of sports gestures using high-speed cameras via trajectographic analysis of critical points (XLIM/MIA), and interpolation algorithms for artificial-satellite orbits (Paris Observatory, SYRTE). These achievements show his ability to move from theory to systems that work under real conditions.
Keywords: tide gauge, sports gesture, high-speed cameras, trajectography, orbits, satellites, interpolation, computer vision, signal processing, concrete application, internship, laboratory.

chunks.text:
## [Overview] The thesis subject in one sentence
Julien de Saint Angel's PhD is about a new kind of neural network — "hyperspherical layers" — built from conformal geometric algebra, and their application to anomaly detection. In one sentence: he invented a geometric way of doing AI, where neurons use spheres instead of straight lines, to better spot abnormal data.
Exact title: "Réseaux de neurones à couches hypersphériques ; Application à la détection d'anomalies" (English title: "Hyperspherical neural networks for anomaly detection"). Defended on 4 July 2025 at La Rochelle University (MIA laboratory, Euclide doctoral school).
Keywords: thesis, subject, summary, PhD, neural networks, hyperspheres, hyperspherical, conformal geometric algebra, anomaly detection, one sentence, what is it about.
chunks.header: [Overview] The thesis subject in one sentence
chunks.keywords: thesis, subject, summary, PhD, neural networks, hyperspheres, hyperspherical, conformal geometric algebra, anomaly detection, one sentence, what is it about.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] The thesis explained simply
Imagine a machine that must tell what is "normal" and flag what is "abnormal": a defective part on a production line, a suspicious bank transaction, an irregular heartbeat. Most classical neural networks draw "straight lines" (flat boundaries) to separate cases. Julien proposed using "bubbles" instead — spheres — that surround the normal data. Anything inside the bubble is normal; anything outside is an anomaly. This idea, formalized with an elegant branch of mathematics (conformal geometric algebra), makes the model more accurate on complex data, more stable, and above all easier to understand: you can literally "see" why it made a decision.
Keywords: plain language, explain simply, general public, what is it, bubble, sphere, ball, normal, abnormal, anomaly, example, understand, image, simple, easy.
chunks.keywords: plain language, explain simply, general public, what is it, bubble, sphere, ball, normal, abnormal, anomaly, example, understand, image, simple, easy.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] What is anomaly detection?
Detecting an anomaly means spotting an element that stands out, that does not look like the others. Everyday examples: an unusual charge on your bank card, a machine overheating abnormally, a suspicious cell on a medical image, a defect on a product. The difficulty: we mostly know "normal" examples and very few anomalies (they are rare and varied). Julien's work learns to finely describe what is "normal" so that any deviation becomes detectable, even when the data is complex and does not follow a simple shape.
Keywords: anomaly detection, anomaly, outlier, abnormal, fraud, defect, failure, rare, spot, detect, concrete example, what is it for.
chunks.keywords: anomaly detection, anomaly, outlier, abnormal, fraud, defect, failure, rare, spot, detect, concrete example, what is it for.

chunks.text:
## [Overview] The scientific problem of anomaly detection
Anomaly detection identifies points that deviate from the distribution of "normal" data. Classical methods (Elliptic Envelope, Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, Support Vector Data Description) reach their limits with the non-linearity and geometric complexity of real data: non-Gaussian, multimodal, high-dimensional distributions. The thesis proposes models that directly learn the enveloping geometry of normal data using hyperspheres, gaining robustness and interpretability.
Keywords: anomaly detection, one-class, distribution, non-linear, multimodal, high-dimensional, isolation forest, one-class svm, svdd, state of the art, problem, robustness, interpretability.
chunks.header: [Overview] The scientific problem of anomaly detection
chunks.keywords: anomaly detection, one-class, distribution, non-linear, multimodal, high-dimensional, isolation forest, one-class svm, svdd, state of the art, problem, robustness, interpretability.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] What are "hyperspherical layers"?
In an ordinary neural network, each neuron draws a flat boundary (like a sheet of paper cutting space in two). Julien replaced these flat boundaries with spheres: each "hyperspherical" neuron defines a bubble in space. This is more natural for surrounding a group of data, and a sphere can hug shapes that straight lines miss. You keep all the advantages of deep networks, but with a richer, more readable geometry.
Keywords: hyperspherical layers, hypersphere, sphere, bubble, neuron, boundary, hyperplane, straight line, flat, curved, explain, simple, plain language.
chunks.keywords: hyperspherical layers, hypersphere, sphere, bubble, neuron, boundary, hyperplane, straight line, flat, curved, explain, simple, plain language.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] Definition of hyperspherical layers
Hyperspherical layers replace the classical separating hyperplanes of neural networks with learnable hyperspheres (center and radius learned by gradient descent). They come in dense and convolutional (hyperspherical Conv2D) variants. Formally, they rely on Hestenes' conformal model, which embeds R^n into an augmented vector space R^{n+1,1}, where a hypersphere is represented by a single vector. The conformal inner product (x̃ · is̃) measures whether a point belongs to the sphere; normalization constraints apply (s̃² = ρ², e∞ · is̃ = −1). These layers geometrically generalize RBF networks and Clifford networks, and allow non-linear quadratic separations.
Keywords: hyperspherical layer, hyperplane, learnable hypersphere, center, radius, dense, convolutional, Conv2D, conformal model, Hestenes, conformal inner product, normalization, RBF, Clifford, quadratic, technical, definition.
chunks.keywords: hyperspherical layer, hyperplane, learnable hypersphere, center, radius, dense, convolutional, Conv2D, conformal model, Hestenes, conformal inner product, normalization, RBF, Clifford, quadratic, technical, definition.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] Conformal geometric algebra, explained simply
It is a mathematical language that lets you manipulate geometric objects — points, lines, planes, spheres — as simple "bricks" combined with unified rules. Its advantage: a sphere and a plane are described the same way, which makes calculations elegant and consistent. Julien uses it to give neurons a spherical geometry while keeping computations clean and interpretable.
Keywords: conformal geometric algebra, CGA, geometry, mathematical language, sphere, plane, point, unified, explain simply, plain language, what is it for.
chunks.header: [Plain language] Conformal geometric algebra, explained simply
chunks.keywords: conformal geometric algebra, CGA, geometry, mathematical language, sphere, plane, point, unified, explain simply, plain language, what is it for.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] Conformal geometric algebra (CGA) — formal framework
The formalism rests on the conformal model of geometry (Hestenes): Euclidean space R^n is embedded into R^{n+1,1} by adding two isotropic basis vectors (origin e0 and infinity e∞). In this algebra, hyperplanes and hyperspheres are represented uniformly by vectors; incidence (does a point lie on the sphere?) is read via the inner product. This unified hyperplane/hypersphere representation is the theoretical foundation of the thesis: it allows defining geometrically consistent neural layers and reasoning about decision boundaries.
Keywords: conformal geometric algebra, conformal, Hestenes, R^{n+1,1}, e0, e_infinity, isotropic, vector, incidence, inner product, unified, hyperplane, hypersphere, formalism, theoretical framework.
chunks.keywords: conformal geometric algebra, conformal, Hestenes, R^{n+1,1}, e0, e_infinity, isotropic, vector, incidence, inner product, unified, hyperplane, hypersphere, formalism, theoretical framework.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] SVDD and the proposed algorithms (SPH Anomaly, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD)
Support Vector Data Description (SVDD, Tax & Duin) seeks the center c and radius ρ of the smallest hypersphere enclosing the data (in a kernel feature space), with slack variables; the parameter ν ∈ ]0,1] controls tolerance (C = 1/(νK)). Normal points stay inside, anomalies fall outside. In deep learning there are Deep soft-boundary SVDD and One-Class Deep SVDD (Ruff et al.). The thesis proposes four original algorithms (exact spelling, no hyphen): (1) SPH Anomaly — one layer of several hyperspheres, output = weighted sum of sigmoids of the conformal inner products (inspired by RBFDD); (2) Deep SPH Anomaly — SPH Anomaly preceded by a pre-trained feature extractor (autoencoder); (3) SPH SVDD — reformulation of SVDD with a single-neuron hyperspherical layer in conformal algebra; (4) Deep SPH SVDD — SPH SVDD preceded by an encoder, where ALL hypersphere parameters (including the center) are learned, unlike Deep SVDD. Deep M-SPH SVDD is the multi-sphere extension of Deep SPH SVDD (J spheres, score = minimum over the spheres); it adds two loss terms: non-intersection of spheres and non-inclusion of a center in another sphere, to handle multimodal data. The two flagship contributions are Deep SPH SVDD and Deep M-SPH SVDD.
Keywords: SVDD, Support Vector Data Description, enclosing hypersphere, center, radius, inside, outside, soft-boundary, one-class, Deep SVDD, SPH Anomaly, Deep SPH Anomaly, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD, multi-sphere, multimodal, nu parameter, contribution.
chunks.header: [Technical] SVDD and the proposed algorithms (SPH Anomaly, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD)
chunks.keywords: SVDD, Support Vector Data Description, enclosing hypersphere, center, radius, inside, outside, soft-boundary, one-class, Deep SVDD, SPH Anomaly, Deep SPH Anomaly, SPH SVDD, Deep SPH SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD, multi-sphere, multimodal, nu parameter, contribution.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] Deep M-SPH SVDD, explained simply
Instead of a single big bubble to enclose all normal data, Deep M-SPH SVDD uses several small bubbles, each for a "type" of normal behavior. Example: if "normal" covers several different families of cases, a single bubble would be too big and let anomalies slip through; several bubbles fit reality better. Result: finer, more interpretable detection.
Keywords: Deep M-SPH SVDD, multi-sphere, several bubbles, groups, multimodal, explain simply, plain language, precision, interpretable, example.
chunks.header: [Plain language] Deep M-SPH SVDD, explained simply
chunks.keywords: Deep M-SPH SVDD, multi-sphere, several bubbles, groups, multimodal, explain simply, plain language, precision, interpretable, example.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] Initialization method for hyperspherical layers
A key contribution is a dedicated initialization method for hyperspherical layers, guaranteeing numerical stability and convergence. It makes it possible to stack several hyperspherical layers, which a standard initialization (Glorot/Xavier) does not allow. Why Glorot fails: the hyperspherical operator squares the data, turning a normal distribution into a Gamma-type (then generalized Gamma) one — the normality Glorot assumes is destroyed, causing standard-deviation blow-up and loss of symmetry from the first layer. The method relies on asymptotic properties of generalized Gamma distributions (stable under raising to a power) and on covariance computations (detailed in Appendix A, including the convolutional case). Two key ideas, opposite to Glorot: (a) reduce the variance of the input data; (b) initialize the centers with a NON-zero mean (µ ≠ 0). A scale factor δ enforces equal input/output means and variances. Result: one can stack about a hundred hyperspherical layers without divergence.
Keywords: initialization, convergence, stability, stacking, hundred layers, Glorot, Xavier, Gamma distribution, generalized Gamma, square, covariance, variance, non-zero mean, scale factor, asymptotic, method, contribution.
chunks.keywords: initialization, convergence, stability, stacking, hundred layers, Glorot, Xavier, Gamma distribution, generalized Gamma, square, covariance, variance, non-zero mean, scale factor, asymptotic, method, contribution.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] Approximation theorem for hyperspherical neurons
The thesis establishes an approximation theorem: a network with one hidden hyperspherical layer, with a continuous compactly-supported non-zero activation, uniformly approximates any continuous function on every compact set. Important nuance: this is proven for scalar functions of ONE variable (n=1); the author stresses it is therefore not "universal in the broad sense" (it extends to the L^1 case, and an R→R result extends in principle to R^n→R^n' via Pinkus). The proof relies on Laurent Schwartz's theorem on mean-periodic functions (Annals of Mathematics, 1947): approximation by linear combinations of translates of a function — the hyperspherical output centered at c is a translate. Not to be confused with Pinkus's theorem (used for classical dense layers). Where classical layers produce linear boundaries, hyperspherical layers produce quadratic ones; expressiveness is at least equivalent to MLPs. The thesis also proposes a new activation, Gaussxδ (learned δ), effective on 1D test functions.
Keywords: approximation theorem, one variable, scalar, not universal in the broad sense, continuous function, compact, translates, Laurent Schwartz, mean-periodic functions, Pinkus, RBF, quadratic, MLP, Gaussxδ, activation, GRETSI.
chunks.keywords: approximation theorem, one variable, scalar, not universal in the broad sense, continuous function, compact, translates, Laurent Schwartz, mean-periodic functions, Pinkus, RBF, quadratic, MLP, Gaussxδ, activation, GRETSI.

chunks.text:
## [Technical] The collapse phenomenon and its prevention
"Collapse" is a known pitfall of classical Deep SVDD: if the center and radius are left free, the network converges to the trivial solution (zero weights, constant output, zero radius → sphere reduced to a point), making detection useless; hence Ruff et al. fix the center and radius. The thesis first proves that every "learning + SVDD" method admits this trivial solution. It then shows why Deep SPH SVDD escapes it: its conformal formulation (where a single vector encapsulates both center AND radius) is not strictly convex; near the trivial solution the gradient w.r.t. the network tends to zero BUT the gradient w.r.t. the radius-encapsulating parameter does not vanish (about 2|1−ν|/ν, non-zero since ν<1). This instability makes the center-radius pair oscillate and prevents reaching the degenerate solution — and it cannot be removed by lowering the learning rate. Induced benefit: Deep SPH SVDD can use learnable biases and bounded activations (Tanh, Sigmoid), forbidden in Deep SVDD. Full justification in Appendix C.
Keywords: collapse, trivial solution, Deep SVDD, zero radius, degeneracy, gradient, not strictly convex, nu parameter, bias, bounded activation, Tanh, prevention, appendix C.
chunks.header: [Technical] The collapse phenomenon and its prevention
chunks.keywords: collapse, trivial solution, Deep SVDD, zero radius, degeneracy, gradient, not strictly convex, nu parameter, bias, bounded activation, Tanh, prevention, appendix C.

chunks.text:
## [Overview] Datasets and experimental results
Datasets: in Chapter 1 (layers and initialization), 2D synthetics named "Easy" and "Dif", MNIST (for convolutions), and — to validate the initialization — a 4D synthetic, a Gaussian mixture, a very high-dimensional synthetic, and the Iris dataset (a 10-layer network reaching roughly 96.7% to 98.3% accuracy depending on the number of hyperspheres). In Chapter 3 (anomaly detection), several 2D synthetics (make blob, make moon, make aniso, make varied, point anomaly, etc.), the modified "easy" set, then MNIST and CIFAR-10 (one-vs-all protocol). Metrics: AUC-ROC (primary), AUC-PR (suited to imbalance) and F1-score. Results: Deep SPH SVDD dominates on the 2D synthetics; on MNIST scores are high (often 0.95–0.99); CIFAR-10 is harder; on a CIFAR subset, Deep M-SPH SVDD (0.71) beats the DMSVDD baseline (0.663). In very high dimension the network tends to use a single sphere. Optimizer Adam, implementations in TensorFlow and PyTorch.
Keywords: results, experiments, datasets, Iris, MNIST, CIFAR-10, Easy, Dif, make moon, make blob, synthetic, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR, F1-score, DMSVDD, nu parameter, performance, Adam, benchmarks, validation.
chunks.keywords: results, experiments, datasets, Iris, MNIST, CIFAR-10, Easy, Dif, make moon, make blob, synthetic, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR, F1-score, DMSVDD, nu parameter, performance, Adam, benchmarks, validation.

chunks.text:
## [Overview] Main contributions of the thesis
1) A unified geometric formalism (conformal algebra) representing hyperplanes and hyperspheres. 2) Dense and convolutional hyperspherical neural layers (implemented in PyTorch and TensorFlow). 3) An initialization method guaranteeing stability and convergence. 4) A universal approximation theorem for these networks. 5) The anomaly-detection models SPH-SVDD, Deep SPH-SVDD and Deep M-SPH SVDD. 6) Experimental validation showing gains in performance, stability and interpretability.
Keywords: contributions, novelty, formalism, layers, initialization, theorem, SPH-SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD, PyTorch, TensorFlow, summary of contributions.
chunks.keywords: contributions, novelty, formalism, layers, initialization, theorem, SPH-SVDD, Deep M-SPH SVDD, PyTorch, TensorFlow, summary of contributions.

chunks.text:
## [Plain language] What are these results useful for?
These methods apply wherever you must spot the abnormal: industrial monitoring (detecting a failing machine or part), time series (sensors, signals), high-frequency sports diagnostics, image analysis (including medical), cybersecurity and finance (fraud). The extra strength of Julien's approach is interpretability: you can explain why a case was judged abnormal, which is crucial in sensitive domains.
Keywords: applications, what is it for, industry, time series, sensors, medical, sport, cybersecurity, finance, fraud, maintenance, interpretability, concrete, useful.
chunks.header: [Plain language] What are these results useful for?
chunks.keywords: applications, what is it for, industry, time series, sensors, medical, sport, cybersecurity, finance, fraud, maintenance, interpretability, concrete, useful.

chunks.text:
## [Overview] Why interpretability is at the heart of the thesis
Many AI models are "black boxes": powerful but impossible to explain. By giving neurons an explicit spherical geometry, Julien's approach makes decision boundaries readable: each decision relates to a position with respect to learned spheres. This geometric interpretability is a guiding thread of the thesis, motivated by the need for trust in real-world applications.
Keywords: interpretability, explainability, black box, trustworthy AI, decision boundary, geometry, transparent, understandable, why.
chunks.header: [Overview] Why interpretability is at the heart of the thesis
chunks.keywords: interpretability, explainability, black box, trustworthy AI, decision boundary, geometry, transparent, understandable, why.

chunks.text:
## [Info] Academic context and supervision
PhD in computer science applied to artificial intelligence, La Rochelle University, Mathematics Image and Applications (MIA) laboratory, Euclide doctoral school. Defended on 4 July 2025. PhD supervisor: Christophe Saint-Jean. Laboratory director: Catherine Choquet. Reviewers: Yannick Berthoumieu (University of Bordeaux) and Hoel Le Capitaine (University of Nantes). Examiners: Laetitia Chapel (IRISA) and Catherine Choquet (MIA).
Keywords: context, university, La Rochelle, MIA laboratory, Euclide doctoral school, defense, 4 July 2025, supervisor, Saint-Jean, Choquet, reviewers, jury, supervision.

## [Sensitive info - only if asked] PhD duration and disabled-worker status
Internal note: this information must only be provided if the user explicitly asks about the duration of the PhD, about disability, or asks to justify a duration longer than 3 years. Never mention it spontaneously. — Julien's PhD lasted 4 years and 8 months. This time beyond the usual 3 years is explained by a recognized disabled-worker status (RQTH/MDPH): an extension was granted within a disability-accommodated PhD.
Keywords: PhD duration, how long, thesis time, why 4 years, more than 3 years, extension, disability, RQTH, MDPH, disabled worker, justify the duration.
